prompt:
I'm building the style inliner for our newsletter pipeline (mail clients ignore <style> blocks, so before send we compute each element's effective style and write it onto the element). The half I keep getting wrong by hand is the cascade itself, so that's the module I want from you: cascade.ts, zero dependencies, TypeScript.

Strong opinion up front: selector MATCHING in this module stays deliberately minimal — single compound selectors only, no combinators, no pseudo-classes. Our selector engine lives elsewhere; this module is about resolving what wins. A compound here is: optional leading * or type name, then any run of .class / #id / [attr] / [attr=value] (value = unquoted [A-Za-z0-9_-]+ or double-quoted, no escapes, no whitespace inside brackets). Class matching is whitespace-token based against attrs.class, id and attribute comparisons are exact. ANYTHING else — combinators (spaces, >, +, ~), commas, pseudo-classes/elements — throws a plain Error, from every export that takes a selector, at parse time, even if the rule would never match.

Shapes (export the types):
  type StyleNode = { tag: string; attrs: Record<string, string>; children: StyleNode[] }
  type Declaration = { prop: string; value: string }
  type Rule = { selector: string; declarations: Declaration[] }
The rules array IS the stylesheet: array order is source order. A declaration value may end with !important (case-insensitive, surrounding whitespace tolerated); emitted values are trimmed with the marker stripped.

Exports:
- specificity(selector: string): [number, number, number] — (id count, class+attribute count, type count). * contributes nothing; repeated simple selectors count every occurrence.
- INHERITED — iterable of the inheritable property names, exactly: color, font-size, font-family, text-align. Everything else (margin, display, background, border...) does not inherit.
- DEFAULTS — { color: "black", "font-size": "16px" }: root-level fallbacks for inheritable properties. font-family and text-align have no default on purpose — absent unless someone sets them.
- computeStyles(root: StyleNode, rules: Rule[]): Map<StyleNode, Record<string, string>> — one entry per element in the tree, keyed by node identity.

Resolution, per element, per property — the part I need to be able to cite in review:
1. Collect every declaration from every rule whose selector matches the element.
2. Pick the winner by, in order: important beats normal; then higher specificity tuple (lexicographic); then later rule in the array; then later declaration within the rule. That last one matters: a rule declaring color twice keeps the second — unless the first was !important and the second isn't.
3. A property with no winning declaration: if it's inheritable, take the parent's computed value (whatever the parent ended up with, verbatim — no unit math, values are opaque strings); at the root, take DEFAULTS when the table has an entry. If it's not inheritable, the property is simply absent from the computed style — callers test with `in`.
So with an empty rule list, every element computes exactly { color: "black", "font-size": "16px" }, and a margin declared on a container shows up on that container only.

Contract: node --test test_cascade.ts must pass unmodified (node 26 type stripping: plain typed TS, no enums/namespaces, keep the ./cascade.ts import extension). The test file is read-only.
